channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oil Industry Lawyers Fight a $50 Billion Climate Case in Oregon
item.description: The lawsuit aims to hold fossil fuel companies responsible for a heat wave in Portland five years ago. The industry says the case should be thrown out.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Michael Hanson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Richard Schechter, a lawyer for Multnomah County in Oregon, addressing the judge in court on Wednesday.

item.title: Gregg Phillips Leaving FEMA
item.description: Gregg Phillips was in charge of the emergency response agency’s largest division and had come under scrutiny for a series of bizarre claims.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Scott Dance
item.media:description: Gregg Phillips was appointed to lead FEMA’s Office of Response and Recovery, with 1,000 employees and a budget of nearly $300 million.

item.title: Trump Administration Forms Partnership to Bank DNA From Imperiled Species
item.description: The government is teaming up with Colossal Biosciences, a private company that claims to have revived extinct dire wolves, to store samples from at-risk animals and plants.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer and Catrin Einhorn
item.media:description: The Frozen Zoo in San Diego, which has been preserving cells for over 50 years. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has announced a partnership with Colossal Biosciences for a similar effort.

item.title: Why Is Europe the Fastest-Warming Continent
item.description: The burning of fossil fuels is raising temperatures worldwide, but local factors, on land and at sea, determine which regions warm most rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: Morning commuters on the London Bridge on Wednesday, where temperatures reached 96 degrees Fahrenheit, the hottest recorded June day in the United Kingdom.

item.title: California Needs Water and Clean Power. It Might Have a Fix for Both.
item.description: A pilot program is building solar panels over irrigation canals to generate electricity. As a bonus, the shade prevents water from evaporating.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:credit: Nathan Weyland for The New York Times

item.title: Heat Wave Takes Toll on Europe’s Infrastructure, Too
item.description: The devastating heat wave has exposed weaknesses in the continent’s infrastructure, much of it built for a cooler climate that no longer exists.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: The Golfech nuclear plant in France on Tuesday after officials shut it down as a result of high temperatures.

item.title: In Europe, Tesla Sales Are Rising Despite Views on Elon Musk
item.description: Price cuts and low-interest-rate loans are luring buyers, including people offended by the company’s chief executive.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: A Tesla Supercharger station in Germany. Tesla’s sales have rebounded sharply in Europe alongside sales of all electric cars.

item.title: Europe’s Scorching Heat Is Gradually Moving East
item.description: Parts of Spain, France and other countries are seeing slight relief, but the record-breaking heat wave is far from over.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel

item.title: As Europe Sweats, Some Politicians Talk of Air-Conditioning, Not Climate Action
item.description: Heat-related deaths and disruptions to daily life are forcing politicians to reckon, in different ways, with a rapidly warming planet.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Carrying a floor fan during the heat in Paris on Thursday.

item.title: How a Bird’s Habitat Can Change Its Song
item.description: For the Bachman’s sparrow, whether a song is passed to the next generation could depend, in part, on the wind and trees.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Climate Change Fueling Europe’s Ferocious Heat Wave, Scientists Find
item.description: A scientific analysis concluded that such high temperatures, across so much of the continent, would “not have been possible” without global warming.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A shady passageway on Tuesday in Bordeaux, France, where temperatures reached 108 degrees Fahrenheit, or roughly 42 Celsius.

item.title: Global Warming Upends London Climate Week
item.description: The extreme heat smothering Europe has upended Climate Week in London.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: King Charles III at a London Climate Week reception on Wednesday at St. James’s Palace.

item.title: Deadly Heat Wave in Europe Continues to Smash Temperature Records
item.description: The continent’s second heat wave since May continued to disrupt daily life for millions. Officials in Spain said it might have contributed to a spike in deaths.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nazaneen Ghaffar, Aurelien Breeden and Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Oscar Del Pozo/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Construction workers in Madrid this week.

item.title: Chemours to Pay $450M in First Federal PFAS Settlement
item.description: The settlement addresses the dumping of PFAS “forever chemicals,” which have been linked to cancer and other risks, by Chemours in several states.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Ed Kashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Chemours plant in Fayetteville, N.C. Under a proposed settlement, Chemours would pay fines for discharging PFAS from plants in North Carolina, New Jersey and West Virginia.

item.title: In London, Extreme Heat Forces Cancellation of Event About Extreme Heat
item.description: Scorching temperatures have disrupted daily life in Britain this week, including events that centered on battling high temperatures.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: Umbrellas protecting from sun, not rain, near London’s Waterloo Station on Wednesday, Britain’s warmest June day on record.

item.title: Traveling to Europe in a Heat Wave? Here’s What to Know.
item.description: Residents and summer travelers alike are searching for ways to stay safe and cool as the continent swelters amid a heat wave. Here are some tips.
item.dc:creator: Ryley Ober
item.media:credit: Joann Pai for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tuesday and Wednesday were the hottest days ever recorded in France.

item.title: These Birds’ Backflips Are Fueled by Sugar
item.description: An ancient dietary change made the manakin’s flashy courtship display possible, a new study suggests.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Saharan Dust Could Add an Apocalyptic Hue to Europe’s Unrelenting Heat
item.description: Powerful winds are carrying dust from Africa as far as southern England, as a heat dome keeps temperatures rising.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:description: Dust from the Sahara in Crete, Greece, in April.

item.title: Deadly Heat Scorches Europe
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nazaneen Ghaffar, Aurelien Breeden and Zane Irwin

item.title: To Escape the Heat, Parisians Jump into an Old Industrial Canal
item.description: Crowds are filling the Canal Saint-Martin in Paris, as temperatures soar above 100 degrees Fahrenheit. Forty people drowned while swimming in other waterways.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Swimming in Canal St. Martin in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: California’s Plastic Law Goes to Court: Is It Too Strict or Too Lax?
item.description: The sweeping legislation, fully enacted last month, has been challenged by Republican-led states. Environmental groups have their own complaints.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:description: California’s law requires that manufacturers reduce the amount of plastic in single-use items such as shampoo bottles by reducing package sizing, switching to a different material or making the products reusable.

item.title: Wildfire Risk in Utah and Across the West Is the Highest It’s Been All Year
item.description: Critical conditions are expected through the weekend across Utah, Arizona, Nevada and elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: The Deseret News, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Residents evacuating as winds drive the Iron Fire toward Eureka, Utah, on Saturday.

item.title: A Climate Chaos Maker
item.description: Global warming is causing extreme heat around the world. Now El Niño, a natural phenomenon that disrupts global weather patterns, is also at work.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Joann Pai for The New York Times
item.media:description: Outside the Louvre museum in Paris, yesterday.

item.title: See Record-Breaking Extreme Heat Wave Across Europe in Photos
item.description: Temperature records have been broken in several European countries this week.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: People cool off under a water mist near the Colosseum, deployed by Italy’s Civil Protection to help residents and tourists cope with the heatwave, in Rome, on Friday.

item.title: Extreme Heat Across Europe Creates Divide Over Leaving Schools Open
item.description: Few schools in Britain or France have air-conditioning to keep children cool in soaring temperatures, leaving parents, teachers and officials divided about what to do.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Students eating ice pops during class at an elementary school in Grays, England, on Wednesday.

item.title: Europe Heat Wave Latest News: After France’s Hottest Day Ever, U.K. Approaches June Record
item.description: More than a dozen countries have issued urgent heat warnings. France saw its highest average temperature ever on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar, Lynsey Chutel and Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: An Influx of Climate Cash
item.description: Many philanthropists are backing away from climate giving. But one is writing very big checks.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Michael Bloomberg, the billionaire and former mayor of New York City, at a climate forum in London on Tuesday.

item.title: Europe’s Heat Has Scientists Asking: How Much Hotter Can It Get?
item.description: Records are being broken for the second time in a month, leading scientists to probe the upper limits of what the warming climate can dish out.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A retirement home in Les Sorinières, near Nantes, France, on Monday, where temperatures reached 108 degrees Fahrenheit.

item.title: Energy Dept. Promises $17.5 Billion in Loans for Nuclear Power
item.description: The federal government wants to help utilities buy large components for up to 10 large nuclear reactors. It’s unclear which, if any, companies might participate.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Vogtle nuclear power plant in Georgia is the only one with AP1000 reactors in the U.S. They generate enough electricity to power 1 million homes, but they took 15 years to build and cost $35 billion, double the initial estimates.

item.title: Former NOAA Employees Revive Climate.gov Web Site
item.description: The database of federal global warming research recreates a website that was closed amid the administration’s broad retreat from climate science.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration headquarters in Silver Spring, Md. The new site is an effort to preserve NOAA work previously available at climate.gov.

item.title: Europe Is Sweltering in an Extreme Heat Wave. Here’s the Latest.
item.description: France’s average temperature on Tuesday was its hottest ever, the country’s weather agency said. Several countries are under top-level heat warnings.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: Europe Created Heat-Wave Protections. Now Comes the ‘Crash Test.’
item.description: Searing temperatures in Western Europe are drawing comparisons to 2003, when a deadly heat wave sparked a reckoning.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: A visitor to the Palace of Versailles in France on Monday, where temperatures exceeded 104 degrees Fahrenheit.

item.title: Interior Department Moves to Relax Rules for Drilling on Public Lands
item.description: A pair of proposals from the Trump administration would lower costs and weaken requirements for fossil fuel companies that drill on federal property.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: One proposal would lower the cost of the bonds that fossil fuel companies must pay the Interior Department before they start drilling or mining on public lands.

item.title: Justice Department Makes It Easier to Bypass Pollution Controls on Pickups
item.description: It has stopped criminal prosecutions of people who install “defeat devices,” which make diesel trucks faster and more efficient but also dirtier.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Acacia Johnson for The New York Times

item.title: What Is the ‘Heat Dome’ Causing Europe’s Record Temperatures?
item.description: Heat domes happen all over the world, and the one over Europe this week is locking in days of near-record highs across Britain, Spain and France.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:description: A swimmer in Paris on Sunday tried to cool off amid Europe’s second major heat wave of the year.

item.title: U.S. Oil Is Skipping the Chance to Grab Market Share From the Gulf
item.description: U.S. oil production is expected to grow only modestly next year as companies hesitate to spend more in an uncertain market.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smaller U.S. oil producers are starting to drill more wells, but big companies like Exxon Mobil and Chevron have said they are not changing their plans as a result of the war with Iran.

item.title: What to Know About Heat in Europe This Week
item.description: Big parts of Europe are under high-level warnings for extreme heat, with forecasters predicting “severe” weather impact.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: Mines, Logistics and Deep Uncertainty Threaten a Middle East Oil Rebound
item.description: More oil is getting out of the Persian Gulf, but the region’s producers are looking for signs that it is safe as they ramp up plans for alternative routes.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels, including oil tankers, anchored this week in the waters off Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: A Humble 3-Wheel Electric Vehicle Lands Toyota in Federal Court
item.description: A lawsuit filed in California claims the automaker’s philanthropic arm stole technology intended to help poor farmers, but it is not clear to what end.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Cynthia R. Matonhodze for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mobility for Africa developed a three-wheeled electric vehicle that rural residents of Zimbabwe use to transport produce and other goods to market.

item.title: Extreme Heat Warnings Issued Across Europe: What to Know
item.description: Unusually high temperatures are expected across parts of Western Europe into early next week, weather agencies said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel

item.title: Is Climate Change Supercharging El Niño?
item.description: As a new, potentially record-breaking El Niño begins, researchers are vigorously debating whether climate change is driving the phenomenon’s intensity.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: Fighting a wildfire in South Sumatra, Indonesia, during an El Niño-exacerbated dry season in 2023.

item.title: Have You Bought a Tesla Recently? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Tesla sales are rebounding in Europe even though many people have negative feelings about Elon Musk, the company’s chief executive. We want to know why.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Tesla’s car sales took a big hit in Europe last year, in part because many people were turned off by the political views of the company’s chief executive, Elon Musk.

item.title: The Shifting Politics of Climate Change
item.description: A new poll suggests Republicans may be more movable on climate change than previously thought, plus more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: While words and phrases like “decarbonization” and climate “crisis” were shown to be ineffective, messages saying that pollution led to rising costs and extreme weather were more likely to persuade U.S. voters.

item.title: Amazon Retaliated Against Workers Who Supported Regulating Data Centers, Complaint Says
item.description: The employees encouraged limits on the complexes in a series of hearings in the tech giant’s hometown, Seattle.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:credit: Jovelle Tamayo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amazon’s campus in Seattle, Wash.

item.title: Trump Administration Backs Off Plan to End Ocean Monitoring System
item.description: The reversal comes after the Senate passed a bipartisan bill on Wednesday to block the removal of deep-sea monitoring instruments.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Darlene Trew Crist/WHOI, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A data-collecting buoy off the Carolina coast. The National Science Foundation was planning to begin removing hundreds of ocean instruments that measure ocean data this month.

item.title: Tropical Storm Arthur Prompts Rare Forecast Warning for the Southeast
item.description: The remnants of Tropical Storm Arthur are raising the risk of flooding across the Gulf Coast.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:description: Floodwaters rose in Galveston, Texas, on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S. Will Waive Oil Sanctions That Have Long Crimped Iran
item.description: The preliminary U.S.-Iran deal temporarily waives restrictions that have limited the country’s oil sales and how much money it has been able to make from those exports.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil facility on Kharg Island, Iran, in 2017.

item.title: Trump Administration to Pay $765 Million to Cancel 4 More Wind Projects
item.description: It’s the third such deal the Interior Department has struck to pay firms to abandon plans for offshore turbines, spending roughly $2.5 billion to get companies to abandon their wind projects.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: The Trump administration has now struck deals energy developers totaling more than $2.5 billion to cancel offshore wind leases.

item.title: Trump’s FEMA Nominee Calls Staff Cuts a ‘Challenge’ for Disaster Agency
item.description: Cameron Hamilton, who briefly led the agency on an acting basis last year but was fired for contradicting the president, also said he would get money out to states faster.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.category: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
item.media:description: Cameron Hamilton, President Trump’s FEMA nominee, testifying before the Senate Homeland Security Committee on Wednesday.

item.title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
item.description: Some shareholders might object, but there is little they could do, legal experts say.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Tesla has sold car and batteries to SpaceX, and the two companies are jointly working on chips and software.

item.title: How a Deal to End the War in Iran Could Affect Energy Prices
item.description: The latest on a tentative deal to end the war in Iran, the Justice Department tries to stop a lawsuit against Elon Musk’s A.I. company and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:description: Even if the hundreds of tankers stuck in the Persian Gulf start moving soon, it will take weeks or months for them to reach their destinations.

item.title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
item.description: The department cited national security concerns, saying Elon Musk’s company had played a crucial role in the Iran war. It also argued it has the authority to stop environmental lawsuits brought by citizens.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction at xAI’s power plant in Southaven, Miss., in February. The NAACP has sued the company over air pollution from generators there.

item.title: New Coral Study Identifies Areas Where Reefs Are Hanging On
item.description: New research has identified areas around the world where cooler currents and other favorable conditions are helping to protect coral from the worst effects of global warming.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Houreld

item.title: Gas Prices May Remain High Despite US-Iran Deal
item.description: The preliminary agreement may not have an immediate effect on prices at the pump. Damaged infrastructure and risky transport could keep costs up.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices have soared since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Algae Is Turning the Reflecting Pool Green. Again.
item.description: Algal blooms have hit the site, between the Lincoln Memorial and the Washington Monument, after a $14.2 million repair project.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: The green water didn't bother some visitors.
